skill.name: agent-framework
skill.description: Build Python agents and multi-agent workflows with Microsoft Agent Framework, Azure AI Foundry, HTTP server mode, and VS Code debugging support.
skill.summary: Scaffold Microsoft Agent Framework agents, workflows, HTTP server wrappers, and local debug configs.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: AI review found no malicious intent and no prompt injection attempt. Most static hits are false positives caused by markdown code fences and inline backticks, but the skill legitimately guides dependency installation, local server execution, MCP tool setup, and .env updates. Publish with a medium-risk warning because these actions can affect the user workspace and network environment.

medium_findings.title: Dependency Installation and Startup Execution
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill instructs agents to install Python packages, generate requirements, and run the created entrypoint in a run-fix loop. This is legitimate for an agent scaffolding skill, but it executes user workspace code and package-manager commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instructions clearly require dependency installation and startup verification. The commands serve the stated development purpose, but they can run code and should be disclosed.

medium_findings.title: MCP Tool Launches External Runtime
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The MCP sample configures Playwright through an npx command pinned to latest and a remote Microsoft Learn MCP endpoint. This can download packages and enable browser or network automation when users adopt the sample.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The MCPStdioTool command and remote MCP URL are explicit in the sample. The use is normal for MCP tooling, but latest package resolution and network access carry supply-chain and capability risks.

medium_findings.title: Workspace Environment File Updates
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill says to always create or update a .env file with Foundry project configuration. It does not request secrets directly, but automated .env writes can overwrite local settings or expose project metadata if committed.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The .env creation and load_dotenv guidance is explicit. It appears operational rather than malicious, but environment files often contain sensitive deployment context.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misread as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Most external command and weak cryptographic algorithm hits come from markdown code fences, inline code, SDK method names, and version strings. They are documentation examples, not hidden Ruby backtick execution or cryptographic code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are markdown prose, tables, or fenced Python examples. No Ruby source, shell interpolation, hashing, or encryption routine is present there.

low_findings.title: Python Syntax Misclassified as Script Risk
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Static hits for deprecated with statements and dynamic imports map to Python async with blocks and ordinary import lists inside code samples. No dynamic import expression or JavaScript with statement was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relevant snippets are static Python imports and context managers. The scanner appears to have treated generic tokens as higher-risk script constructs.

low_findings.title: Local Debug Ports and Microsoft URL Are Documented Configuration
low_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW: The hardcoded IP and URL hits are local debug listener addresses and a Microsoft Learn MCP endpoint. They are expected for debugging and documentation access, but users should review exposed local ports.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The destinations are visible and purpose-aligned. The remaining risk depends on how generated debug configurations are used on the user machine.

dangerous_patterns.title: Package Manager Command in Generated MCP Tool
dangerous_patterns.description: The sample uses an MCP stdio tool with command npx and an argument that resolves the latest Playwright MCP package. This pattern should be reviewed before execution in sensitive workspaces.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command and package argument are directly present in the sample. It is a legitimate MCP setup pattern, but it runs external package-manager code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Automated Server Startup Verification
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow asks the agent to run generated code until startup succeeds, then stop the server. This can execute user-created code and bind local ports during validation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The run-fix loop and local server commands are explicit. The instruction includes cleanup, which lowers but does not remove execution risk.

content.value_statement: Teams need reliable scaffolding for Python agents, workflows, server mode, and debugging. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Microsoft Agent Framework patterns.
actual_capabilities: Scaffolds Python agents using Microsoft Agent Framework SDK patterns.
Builds multi-agent workflows with executors, handlers, edges, and streaming output.
Adds HTTP server mode with Azure AI Agent Server SDK wrappers.
Creates VS Code debug tasks and launch configurations for Agent Inspector.
Generates requirements files with pinned Agent Framework and server packages.
Documents setup, configuration, and local run instructions.
limitations: It does not deploy agents to Azure or Microsoft Foundry.
It does not evaluate agent quality or safety outcomes.
It assumes Python and the Microsoft Agent Framework SDK.
It may need live Azure project details and local package installation.

use_cases.title: Create a new Foundry-backed agent
use_cases.description: Generate a Python agent with model configuration, tools, server mode, and setup documentation.

use_cases.title: Prototype a multi-agent workflow
use_cases.description: Use executor and handler patterns to connect writer, reviewer, student, or teacher style agents.

use_cases.title: Prepare local debugging for an agent service
use_cases.description: Create VS Code tasks and launch settings for Agent Inspector and debugpy workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic agent
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a single agent
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Microsoft Agent Framework Python agent for my project. Use placeholders for Foundry configuration and include setup steps.

prompt_templates.title: Add server and debugging
prompt_templates.prompt: Build an Agent Framework agent with HTTP server mode, VS Code debug configs, and a README for local testing.

prompt_templates.title: Build a workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a multi-agent workflow with separate writer and reviewer nodes, streaming output, and clear handler types.

prompt_templates.title: Integrate tools and Foundry
prompt_templates.prompt: Scaffold a Foundry-backed Agent Framework workflow with MCP tool setup, pinned dependencies, server mode, and startup verification.

output_examples.input: Create a Foundry-backed support agent with local debugging.
output_examples.output: A Python project structure with an agent entrypoint, environment placeholders, pinned dependencies, VS Code debug settings, and setup documentation.

output_examples.input: Build a writer and reviewer workflow.
output_examples.output: A multi-agent workflow design with typed executor nodes, streaming event handling, server-ready structure, and run instructions.

output_examples.input: Add HTTP server mode to my existing agent.
output_examples.output: Updated agent startup behavior, server wrapper guidance, required packages, and notes for local Agent Inspector testing.

best_practices: Use a virtual environment and pinned package versions before running generated agents.
Review .env changes before committing or sharing the workspace.
Keep server mode and debug ports local unless deployment is explicitly requested.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill for Azure deployment or production release automation.
Do not run MCP tools or package installs without reviewing the generated commands.
Do not store secrets in generated examples or README files.

faq.question: What does this skill create?
faq.answer: It creates Python Agent Framework agents, workflows, server wrappers, debug configs, requirements, and setup documentation.

faq.question: Does it deploy agents to Azure?
faq.answer: No. It prepares local code and configuration. Use a deployment-specific skill or workflow for Azure deployment.

faq.question: Can it build multi-agent workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes patterns for executor nodes, handler signatures, streaming events, and loop-based workflows.

faq.question: Does it need Azure credentials?
faq.answer: Generated projects may use Azure identity at runtime. The skill should not store credentials in files.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill may install packages, update .env files, run local servers, and configure MCP tools. These actions are legitimate but sensitive.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
